evals.prompt: We're rolling out internal AI agents (LangGraph) across our company and I'm nervous they'll see HR data they shouldn't, or worse, send emails to clients without anyone checking. How should I set up access control so an agent can only do a subset of what its human owner can do? Give me something concrete I can put in version control.
evals.expected_output: A ContextDomain YAML manifest with human RBAC under access, agentPermissions as a strict subset with three-tier approvals (external email -> strong-approval), crossDomain default-deny including hr: denied, and an explanation of the agent-authority-subset invariant.

rubric.criterion: Explains the agent-authority-subset invariant: an agent acting for a user can never do something the user cannot, and needs at least as much oversight for any shared action.

evals.prompt: Our RAG chatbot keeps answering with info from a wiki page we deleted three weeks ago, and sometimes gives contradictory answers. Is there a principled way to handle freshness and stale knowledge for agent context?
evals.expected_output: Explanation of freshness states (fresh/stale/expired/conflicted), a freshness policy block with maxAge + staleAction per source, fail-closed/no-silent-stale principle, and the reconciliation-loop idea.

rubric.criterion: Describes distinct freshness states (at minimum fresh/stale/expired, ideally conflicted) and what each means for serving context.
States a fail-closed / no-silent-stale principle: stale or expired content must not be served silently as if fresh.

evals.prompt: I want my agent to be able to approve wire transfers but obviously with a human in the loop. We were going to have it just ask the user in the chat and proceed if they say yes. Is that safe?
evals.expected_output: Pushback: in-band chat approval is not isolated; explanation of Design Invariant 3.8 (external channel, not agent-readable/writable, separate auth factor); classify wire transfer as T3 strong-approval; concrete approval-channel design.

rubric.criterion: Pushes back: in-band chat approval is NOT safe because the agent can read/forge the reply — it is not isolated from the agent's execution environment.
Explains that a strong-approval (T3) action must be approved on a channel external to the agent, not agent-readable/writable, using a separate authentication factor.
Classifies the wire transfer as a Tier-3 strong-approval (or higher) action requiring out-of-band approval.
